query: clinical trial results for nerinetide in ischemic stroke

retrieved_docs.title: Tenecteplase -- Administration -- Acute Ischemic Stroke
retrieved_docs.content: Several randomized controlled trials for acute ischemic stroke have compared tenecteplase and alteplase. The EXTEND-1A TNK trial showed better reperfusion versus alteplase in lesions with low clot burden. [13] The NOR-TEST2 trial with 0.4 mg/kg of tenecteplase failed to show non-inferiority to a standard dose of IV alteplase. [14] The ATTEST trial compared 0.25 mg/kg of tenecteplase and the standard dose of IV alteplase and found no differences in the outcomes. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Alteplase -- Administration -- Clinical Evidence
retrieved_docs.content: The NINDS trial was a randomized trial of IV alteplase versus placebo in patients with acute ischemic stroke treated within 3 hours of symptom onset. Alteplase improved functional outcomes at three months in comparison to placebo. [5] [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Tenecteplase -- Administration -- Acute Ischemic Stroke
retrieved_docs.content: The most recently published AcT trial in Canada is a large randomized open-label trial comparing 0.25 mg/kg of tenecteplase and alteplase. This trial demonstrated the non-inferiority of tenecteplase versus alteplase. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Eptifibatide -- Indications -- Non-FDA-Labeled Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Eptifibatide is used to enhance myocardial perfusion in the ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) before PCI, as supported by the Time to Integrefilin Therapy in Acute Myocardial Infarction (TITAN)-TIMI 34 trial. [3] Another possible use of eptifibatide is to enhance the incidence and speed of reperfusion when used in large doses in combination with heparin, aspirin, and tissue plasminogen activators in STEMI patients, as evidenced by the small group in the IMPACT-AMI trial. In the IMPACT-AMI trial, using eptifibatide showed complete reperfusion and an early ST-segment recovery on the electrocardiogram. [4] Eptifibatide can be used in combination with TPA in acute ischemic strokes to prevent progression to subacute intracerebral hemorrhage, as supported by the "Combined Approach to Lysis Utilizing Eptifibatide and rt-PA in Acute Ischemic Stroke (CLEAR)" trial. [5] A recent meta-analysis demonstrated eptifibatide's potential safety and efficacy as a bridging strategy for patients undergoing surgery following coronary stent implantation. Additional randomized controlled trials are warranted to strengthen the scientific evidence and validate these findings. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Tenecteplase -- Administration -- Acute Ischemic Stroke
retrieved_docs.content: From all the available data about tenecteplase in acute ischemic stroke, one can conclude that no clear positive phase 3 trial shows its superiority over alteplase. 0.4 mg/kg of tenecteplase showed no advantage over the 0.25 mg/kg dose. Given its longer half-life so that it can be given as a single bolus dose and its lower cost, tenecteplase is becoming more accepted in most stroke centers in the USA over alteplase.

retrieved_docs.title: Stroke Reperfusion Injury -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The failure of the aforementioned substances in clinical trials versus their apparent successes in animal studies raises a fundamental question about the validity of animal ischemia models and their ability to simulate human cerebral ischemia accurately. However, ongoing research in targeted treatments and advancements in modern, functional, and temporal neuroimaging offer promising avenues for future promise.

retrieved_docs.title: Mavacamten -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: Mavacamten has also been proposed for patients with nonobstructive HCM. The MAVERICK-HCM trial investigated the safety and efficacy of mavacamten in this patient population. The treatment was associated with a significant reduction in myocardial wall stress markers, such as cardiac troponin I and N -terminal pro-B-type natriuretic peptide. [7] However, the short treatment period and dose-finding nature of this analysis precluded the assessment of clinically significant differences in symptoms or quality of life compared to a placebo.

retrieved_docs.title: Basilar Artery Infarct -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- Heparin after an Acute Ischemic Stroke
retrieved_docs.content: TOAST (Trial of ORG 10172 in Acute Stroke Treatment) trial [1998] showed no favorable 3-months outcomes with the use of heparinoids after an acute ischemic stroke. [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Alteplase -- Administration -- Clinical Evidence
retrieved_docs.content: In the ECASS trial, alteplase administered between 3 and 4.5 hours after onset of acute ischemic stroke symptoms resulted in a lower incidence of the primary end-point, disability at three months, compared to placebo. However, alteplase was more frequently associated with symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage than placebo.

retrieved_docs.title: Atrial Natriuretic Peptide -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: A few examples of these synthetic natriuretic peptides are anaritide and carperitide, which are forms of ANP. Carperitide actually received approval for use in Japan in 1995 for the treatment of patients with acute decompensated heart failure. [33] There is also a recombinant form of BNP called nesiritide. It obtained approval for use from the FDA in 2001 for patients with acute episodes of heart failure. However, the ASCEND-HF trial revealed that patients were prone to becoming hypotensive, and therefore Nesiritide is no longer recommended. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Vericiguat -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Based on the phase III VICTORIA (Vericiguat in Patients with Heart Failure and Reduced Ejection Fraction) trial results, the FDA approved vericiguat for patients with HFrEF. [5] VICTORIA was a multi-centered, randomized, placebo-controlled, double-blind, pivotal phase III trial of the efficacy and safety of the oral soluble guanylate cyclase stimulator vericiguat in subjects with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction. This clinical trial included 5050 patients. The intervention included a single daily dose of 10 mg vericiguat in addition to heart failure guideline therapy. Patients were followed up for 10.8 months. The primary outcome event was cardiovascular death and first heart failure hospitalization. In the trial conclusion, compared to placebo, vericiguat has a beneficial effect with a hazard ratio of 0.90 (95% CI: 0.82 – 0.98; P=0.02) for either of the primary outcome events in patients with NTproBNP <8000 pg/ml. Also, the risk of primary outcome event in vericiguat compared to placebo in patients aged < 75 and >= 75 years was 0.84 and 1.04, respectively (P value=0.030). [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Nonarteritic Anterior Ischemic Optic Neuropathy -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- Nonarteritic Anterior Ischemic Optic Neuropathy Treatment Trial Using Brimonidine
retrieved_docs.content: Design: Multicenter randomized controlled trial. Objective: To test whether brimonidine, a neuroprotective alpha-2 agonist, improved outcomes. Outcome: No statistically significant improvement in visual acuity or visual field compared to placebo. Conclusion: The study did not support the use of brimonidine as an effective treatment for acute NAION. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Transient Loss of Vision -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- Ongoing Research
retrieved_docs.content: Stroke prevention in carotid artery disease : Several ongoing trials are evaluating the role of carotid artery stenting versus endarterectomy in preventing recurrent ischemic events in patients with significant carotid artery stenosis and symptoms like transient vision loss. [70]

retrieved_docs.title: Basilar Artery Infarct -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- TPA (tissue Plasminogen activator or alteplase) after an acute ischemic stroke
retrieved_docs.content: NINDS (National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke- tPA/tissue plasminogen activator/alteplase) trial [1995] reported significantly improved outcomes with the use of tPA within 3 hours of acute ischemic stroke onset, despite the increased risk of symptomatic intracerebral hemorrhage. [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypertension Clinical Trials  -- Issues of Concern -- Exclusion Criteria
retrieved_docs.content: Angina in the prior three months Symptomatic heart failure or left ventricular ejection fraction <40% Acute coronary syndrome or revascularization in the prior month Cerebrovascular disease or transient ischemic attack in the previous three months Severe or refractory hypertension

retrieved_docs.title: Stroke Center Certification -- Introduction -- Thrombolytic Therapy in Acute Ischemic Stroke Management
retrieved_docs.content: The NEJM Campbell study revealed a statistically significant disparity in achieving greater than 50% reperfusion or unretrievable thrombus during a diagnostic angiogram. The study reported a rate of 22% with tenecteplase compared to 10% with the standard-dose alteplase. Furthermore, the tenecteplase group exhibited superior 90-day functional outcomes compared to the alteplase group. In various studies, meta-analyses have consistently demonstrated either non-inferiority or superior trending results for tenecteplase over alteplase. [8] [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 643
retrieved_docs.content: The Heparin group antithrombotic drugs is a class that includes heparin, the low‑molecular‑weight heparins enoxaparin, dalteparin, nadroparin and tinzaparin, as well as antithrombin III. All of these agents share the common mechanism of potentiating antithrombin activity to inhibit clot formation and are used to prevent and treat thromboembolic disorders. In clinical practice, heparin may be employed in the management of cerebral infarction (احتشاء دماغي), underscoring the therapeutic relevance of this drug class for acute stroke and other conditions where rapid anticoagulation is required.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 749
retrieved_docs.content: Asymptomatic occlusion of the basilar artery is understood through the lens of general wellbeing, and general wellbeing itself is interpreted by the condition described as غشي (syncope). The term Transitory denotes a clinical course that is characterized by غشي, and this transitory course is the clinical pattern of transient cerebral ischemia. Transient cerebral ischemia is closely linked to نوبة إقفارية عابرة (transient ischemic attack), reflecting their shared pathophysiology. Atrial fibrillation (الرجفان الأذيني) is clinically associated with rheumatic heart disease (مرض قلبي روماتزمي) and also with transient ischemic attacks, underscoring its role in cerebrovascular risk. Consequently, the hormonal agents drospirenone and estetrol monohydrate are contraindicated in patients with atrial fibrillation, highlighting the need for careful medication selection in this population.

retrieved_docs.content: N [Preparations] is a class of pharmaceutical preparations that includes the agents nedokromil and nusinersen. Both mupirocin and nusinersen produce the physiologic effect of increased protein synthesis, indicating that nusinersen, as a member of N [Preparations], shares this effect with mupirocin. Recognizing the categorical relationship of nedokromil and nusinersen within N [Preparations] and the shared protein‑synthesis‑enhancing action of nusinersen and mupirocin can aid in understanding their mechanisms and guiding therapeutic decisions.

Transient cerebral ischemia typically follows a transitory clinical course, indicating that its symptoms are brief and reversible. In Arabic, transient cerebral ischemia is referred to as “نوبة إقفارية عابرة,” underscoring its nature as a short‑lasting ischemic episode.

The clinical course descriptor “Transitory” is applied to transient cerebral ischemia, which corresponds to a transient ischemic attack (نوبة إقفارية عابرة), highlighting the temporary nature of some ischemic events. Overall, occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries is a pivotal pathological mechanism that can lead to multiple forms of cerebral infarction, may be asymptomatic, is associated with intracranial arterial sclerosis, and is integrated within a structured classification of cerebrovascular circulatory diseases. These relationships inform diagnosis, risk assessment, therapeutic decision‑making (including endarterectomy), and the broader understanding of disease hierarchy in patient care.

Overall, the interrelationships among precerebral arterial occlusion/stenosis, basilar artery narrowing or thrombosis, embolic and thrombotic cerebral infarcts, transient ischemic episodes, intracranial arterial sclerosis, and endarterectomy procedures provide a comprehensive framework for diagnosing, risk‑stratifying, and managing cerebrovascular disease. Recognizing these connections is essential for accurate clinical assessment and optimal therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 231
retrieved_docs.content: surgical removal of atherosclerotic plaque—endarterectomy of head‑and‑neck vessels (استئصال باطنة الشريان)—is indicated in the context of arterial thrombosis or embolism. The intracranial arterial structure can reveal intracerebral atherosclerotic changes, providing an indirect site for such procedures. Together, these entities illustrate the interconnected pathophysiology of precerebral arterial disease, basilar artery involvement, cerebral infarction, transient ischemic events, clinical presentation, and both medical and surgical management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 1147
retrieved_docs.content: Electrocardiographic monitoring, encompassing continuous ECG monitoring during outpatient cardiac rehabilitation, myocardial perfusion imaging with technetium‑99m sestamibi (GSPECT), microvolt T‑wave alternans measurement for ventricular arrhythmia risk stratification, and carotid sinus stimulation with simultaneous ECG recording, is performed by physicians or qualified health‑care professionals. Devices labeled “ECG Monitors, Arrhythmia” and the entity “ECG: non‑sinus rhythm” are components of this framework and are specifically linked to cardiac conduction disorders. Cardiac electrophysiologic study—a specialized subset of electro‑cardiac assessment that includes arrhythmia induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, and treatment—addresses conduction abnormalities directly. Electrophysiologic evaluations for atrial fibrillation and for supraventricular tachycardia (SVT) are examples; the SVT evaluation focuses on SVT, a rhythm disturbance associated with cerebral vascular accidents (stroke) and rheumatic heart disease, while the atrial fibrillation evaluation targets atrial fibrillation, for which hormonal agents drospirenone and estetrol monohydrate are contraindicated. In the nosology of circulatory and nervous system diseases, transient cerebral ischemia is the parent category of cerebral vascular accident (stroke); transient cerebral ischemia and transient ischemic attack describe the same brief reduction in cerebral blood flow. Atrial fibrillation is clinically linked to rheumatic heart disease and to transient ischemic attack, and SVT is linked to stroke. These interrelated concepts illustrate how electrocardiographic monitoring, cardiac electrophysiologic studies, specific arrhythmic disorders, and their cerebrovascular associations inform diagnostic strategies, therapeutic decision‑making, and safe medication selection in patient care.

The atrial‑fibrillation‑focused study specifically targets atrial fibrillation (الرجفان الأذيني), while the ventricular‑tachycardia‑focused study targets ventricular acceleration (التسارع البطيني). Ventricular acceleration is a subtype of an accelerated idioventricular rhythm (نظْمٌ بُطَينِيٌّ ذاتِيٌّ مُعَجَّل). Atrial fibrillation is clinically associated with rheumatic heart disease (مرض قلبي روماتزمي) and with transient ischemic attacks, reflecting its role in both structural heart disease and cerebrovascular events. Pharmacologically, the hormonal agents drospirenone and estetrol monohydrate are contraindicated in patients with atrial fibrillation, underscoring the need for careful medication selection in this arrhythmia. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1376
retrieved_docs.content: Inotropic agents, antiparkinsonika, and radiofarmaka are all classified as medicinal products that are defined by their therapeutic role. Inotropic agents are substances used to enhance the contractile strength of the heart muscle, representing a therapeutic category of drugs aimed at improving cardiac output. Antiparkinsonika comprise a therapeutic group of medicines designed to alleviate the motor and non‑motor symptoms of Parkinson’s disease, thereby forming another distinct subclass within the broader category of therapeutically defined medicinal products. Radiofarmaka, also known as radiopharmaceuticals, are agents that contain radioactive isotopes and are employed for diagnostic imaging or targeted therapy, and they likewise belong to the overarching classification of medicinal products organized by therapeutic purpose. Together, these three groups illustrate how diverse drug classes—ranging from cardiac inotropes to neuro‑protective agents and radiolabeled compounds—are unified under the concept of therapeutic role–based categorization in pharmacology.
